nodes.name: Board Games & Card Games
nodes.description: Classic board games, card games, and tabletop gaming

items.prompt: Which strategy game, originating in 6th-century India as chaturanga, is played on a 64-square board with pieces including knights, bishops, and rooks?
items.explanation:
Originated in India c. 6th century CE as chaturanga; spread to Persia (shatranj), then to Europe by c. 1000 CE
Played on an 8x8 board with 16 pieces per side; the goal is to checkmate the opponent's king
FIDE (Fédération Internationale des Échecs) founded in 1924; administers the World Championship since 1948
Garry Kasparov became youngest undisputed world champion in 1985 at age 22
IBM's Deep Blue defeated Kasparov in 1997 — first time a computer beat a reigning world champion under standard time controls
Bobby Fischer's 1972 victory over Boris Spassky in Reykjavik ('Match of the Century') was a Cold War cultural event
The Lewis Chessmen (c. 12th century), found on Scotland's Isle of Lewis, are among the most famous archaeological artifacts in Europe

items.prompt: Which ancient strategy game, one of the four cultivated arts of Chinese scholars, is played by placing black and white stones on a 19x19 grid to surround territory?
items.explanation:
Originated in China over 2,500 years ago; earliest textual reference in the Zuo Zhuan (c. 548 BCE)
Known as weiqi (Chinese), baduk (Korean), and igo (Japanese)
One of the four cultivated arts of the Chinese scholar-gentleman, alongside calligraphy, painting, and guqin
Played on a 19x19 grid (361 intersections); players place stones to surround territory; captured stones are removed
Google DeepMind's AlphaGo defeated world champion Lee Sedol 4–1 in Seoul in March 2016 — a landmark moment for AI
Has more possible board positions than atoms in the observable universe (approximately 2.1 x 10^170)
Original board was 17x17; the 19x19 standard was established during the Tang dynasty (618–907 CE)
items.cardFront: Weiqi/baduk, black and white stones, 19x19 grid, territory

items.prompt: Which word game, invented by an unemployed American architect during the Great Depression, uses letter tiles with point values based on frequency analysis of the New York Times?
items.explanation:
Invented by Alfred Mosher Butts in 1938 (evolved from his earlier game Lexiko/Criss-Crosswords)
Butts studied the front page of the New York Times to calculate letter frequency and assign point values
James Brunot bought the rights in 1948, simplified the rules, and renamed it Scrabble
The blank tile is worth 0 points; Q and Z are worth 10 points each; there are 100 tiles in the English edition
New Zealander Nigel Richards is the most dominant competitive player — five-time World Champion
Richards also won the French-language World Championship in 2015 despite not speaking French; he memorized the French dictionary
The highest possible single-word score is 1,778 points for OXYPHENBUTAZONE (theoretically, across three triple-word squares)
items.cardFront: Letter tiles, crossword-style board, Alfred Mosher Butts

items.prompt: Which real-estate trading game was originally derived from The Landlord's Game, a 1904 creation designed to illustrate the economic theories of Henry George?
items.explanation:
Based on The Landlord's Game, patented in 1904 by Elizabeth 'Lizzie' Magie to demonstrate Georgist economics
Charles Darrow encountered a modified version, redrew it, and sold it to Parker Brothers in 1935 — marketed as sole inventor
Magie sold her patent to Parker Brothers for $500 in 1935; received no royalties
The standard U.S. board uses Atlantic City street names; Boardwalk and Park Place are the most expensive properties
Ralph Anspach's Anti-Monopoly lawsuit in the 1970s uncovered the true origin story
Over 275 million games sold worldwide; translated into 47+ languages
The game's iron, top hat, and Scottie dog tokens are cultural icons; the thimble was retired in 2017
items.cardFront: Real-estate trading, Lizzie Magie's Landlord's Game, Atlantic City streets

items.prompt: Which global conquest game, originally called La Conquete du Monde, was invented by the French filmmaker who also directed The Red Balloon?
items.explanation:
Invented in 1957 by Albert Lamorisse, the French filmmaker who directed The Red Balloon (1956, Palme d'Or)
Originally titled La Conquete du Monde ('The Conquest of the World'); produced by Miro Company in France
Parker Brothers acquired the rights and published it as Risk in 1959 with revised rules
Players deploy armies across a world map divided into 42 territories and 6 continents
Combat is resolved by dice rolls; attacking with more dice gives a statistical advantage
Inspired later strategy games including Axis & Allies and Settlers of Catan
Australia (Oceania) is famously the easiest continent to defend — only one entry point from Siam
items.cardFront: La Conquete du Monde, Albert Lamorisse, global conquest, dice combat

items.prompt: Which German-style resource-trading game, set on a modular hex-tile island, launched the modern board game renaissance when it won the Spiel des Jahres in 1995?
items.explanation:
Designed by Klaus Teuber; published in 1995 in Germany; won the Spiel des Jahres (Game of the Year) that year
Players collect and trade resources (wood, brick, ore, grain, wool) to build settlements, cities, and roads
Modular hex-tile board means every game has a different map layout
Credited with launching the 'Eurogame' or modern board game movement in North America
Over 40 million copies sold worldwide; translated into 40+ languages
Officially rebranded from 'The Settlers of Catan' to simply 'Catan' in 2015
Klaus Teuber died on April 1, 2023, at age 70; he was a former dental technician who became a full-time game designer
items.cardFront: Hex-tile island, resource trading, Spiel des Jahres 1995, Klaus Teuber

items.prompt: Which race game, played with 15 checkers per side on a board of 24 narrow triangles called points, has ancestors dating back approximately 5,000 years to Mesopotamia?
items.explanation:
One of the oldest known board games; ancestors trace to approximately 3000 BCE in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq)
Played on a board with 24 triangular 'points'; each player has 15 checkers and moves according to dice rolls
The doubling cube, introduced in 1920s New York, revolutionized the gambling and strategic aspects of the game
Edmund Hoyle published the first standardized rules in 1743
The name 'backgammon' first appears in English in 1635 in a letter by James Howell
A member of the 'tables' family of games, which includes Roman ludus duodecim scriptorum and tabula
The World Backgammon Championship has been held annually since 1979
items.cardFront: 24 points, 15 checkers, doubling cube, Mesopotamian ancestors

items.prompt: Which card game, descended from the French game poque, became synonymous with Mississippi riverboat gambling in the 1830s and now features a World Series held annually in Las Vegas?
items.explanation:
Developed in the early 19th-century United States; derives its name from French poque and German pochen ('to knock')
First documented in New Orleans in 1829, initially played with a 20-card deck among four players
Expanded to a 52-card deck between 1830 and 1850; straights and flushes added during this period
The first World Series of Poker (WSOP) was held in 1970 at Binion's Horseshoe in Las Vegas, organized by Benny Binion
Texas Hold'em is the most popular modern variant; each player gets two hole cards plus five community cards
The 'Dead Man's Hand' (black aces and eights) was supposedly held by Wild Bill Hickok when he was shot in 1876
Chris Moneymaker's 2003 WSOP Main Event win (via an $86 online satellite) triggered the modern poker boom
items.cardFront: Poque origin, riverboat gambling, WSOP, Texas Hold'em

items.prompt: Which four-player partnership card game, played with a standard 52-card deck, features a bidding auction, a dummy hand, and a contract to win a certain number of tricks?
items.explanation:
Contract bridge was codified by Harold S. Vanderbilt in 1925, evolving from auction bridge and earlier whist
Played by two partnerships of two; bidding determines the contract (number of tricks to win and trump suit)
One hand (the 'dummy') is laid face-up on the table after the opening lead; the declarer plays both hands
Ely Culbertson popularized the game in the 1930s through flamboyant publicity and his Bridge World magazine
Charles Goren's 4–3–2–1 point-count system (aces=4, kings=3, etc.) became the standard bidding evaluation method
Omar Sharif, the film star of *Lawrence of Arabia* and *Doctor Zhivago*, was an avid bridge player and columnist
The Bermuda Bowl (world team championship) has been held since 1950; Italy's Blue Team won 10 times between 1957–1975
items.cardFront: Partnership card game, bidding, dummy hand, contract, tricks

items.prompt: Which trivia-themed board game, invented by two Canadian journalists after a missing piece from their Scrabble set inspired a new idea, uses pie-shaped playing pieces that collect colored wedges?
items.explanation:
Invented on December 15, 1979, in Montreal by Chris Haney (photo editor, The Gazette) and Scott Abbott (sports editor, Canadian Press)
First commercially released in Canada in 1981; 1,100 copies produced for the initial run at $15 each
Licensed to Selchow & Righter (U.S. publisher of Scrabble) in 1982; became a massive hit
Six categories in the original Genus edition: Geography, Entertainment, History, Art & Literature, Science & Nature, Sports & Leisure
Players move around a wheel-shaped board collecting colored wedges ('pie pieces') by answering questions correctly
Sold over 100 million copies worldwide; acquired by Hasbro when it bought Selchow & Righter
Fred L. Worth sued the creators in 1984, claiming they used facts from his trivia encyclopedias — the case was dismissed because facts cannot be copyrighted
items.cardFront: Canadian invention, six categories, colored wedges, pie-shaped tokens

items.prompt: Which deduction game, invented during WWII blackouts, challenges players to determine a killer, weapon, and room by making accusations like 'Colonel Mustard in the library with the candlestick'?
items.explanation:
Invented in 1943 by Anthony E. Pratt, a British solicitor's clerk, during WWII air-raid blackouts
Patented in 1947; first manufactured by Waddingtons in the UK in 1949 as Cluedo (a portmanteau of 'Clue' and Latin 'ludo,' meaning 'I play')
Sold in North America as Clue by Parker Brothers; the victim is Mr. Boddy (Mr. Black in the UK edition)
Original six suspects: Miss Scarlet, Colonel Mustard, Mrs. White, Reverend Green, Mrs. Peacock, Professor Plum
Six weapons in the classic edition: candlestick, knife, lead pipe, revolver, rope, wrench
The 1985 film *Clue* starring Tim Curry was released with three different endings shown in different theaters
Players use deductive reasoning to identify which suspect, weapon, and room cards are hidden in the envelope
items.cardFront: Whodunit, six suspects, six weapons, nine rooms, deduction

items.prompt: Which tile-based game, developed in mid-19th-century China, sparked a massive American craze in the 1920s after Abercrombie & Fitch began importing sets?
items.explanation:
Developed in the mid-to-late 1800s in China's Yangtze River Delta region; descended from earlier Chinese draw-and-discard card games
Played with 144 tiles featuring suits (bamboo, circles, characters), winds, dragons, and bonus tiles
Four players draw and discard tiles to form sets (pungs, kongs, chows) and complete a winning hand
Abercrombie & Fitch began selling sets in the U.S. in 1920; Joseph Park Babcock published simplified American rules the same year
Became a nationwide craze in 1920s America; department stores offered in-store classes
The National Mah Jongg League (founded 1937) standardizes American rules and publishes an annual card with new hands
Strongly associated with Jewish-American culture; became a staple of Jewish women's social gatherings from the mid-20th century onward
items.cardFront: 144 tiles, Chinese origin, 1920s American craze, four players
